content:
# Mark Chapter 16 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Mark Chapter 16 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 16:1 — "bought spices" vs. "bought burial spices" for ἠγόρασαν ἀρώματα

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "bought spices in-order-that having-come they-might-anoint him" | Literal; ἀρώματα = spices/aromatics. |
| LOGOS | "bought spices, so that they might go and anoint him" | Formal; purpose clause preserved. |
| PNEUMA | "bought burial spices so they could go and anoint Jesus's body" | Dynamic; adds "burial" and "Jesus's body" for clarity. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "bought spices, so that they might go and anoint him" — ἀρώματα simply means "spices" or "aromatic substances." PNEUMA's "burial spices" and "Jesus's body" are contextually accurate additions but not in the Greek. Mark's readers understand the purpose from context.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 16:1 — "When the Sabbath was past" vs. "When the Sabbath was over" for Διαγενομένου τοῦ σαββάτου

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "when the Sabbath passed" | Literal; genitive absolute. |
| LOGOS | "When the Sabbath was past" | Formal; slightly more archaic. |
| PNEUMA | "When the Sabbath was over" | Dynamic; modern English idiom. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "When the Sabbath was past" — "was past" has a more literary quality appropriate to the narrative register. "Was over" (PNEUMA) is colloquially natural but slightly flat for the momentous transition from Sabbath rest to resurrection morning.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 16:2 — "when the sun had risen" vs. "just after sunrise" for ἀνατείλαντος τοῦ ἡλίου

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the sun having-risen" | Literal; genitive absolute. |
| LOGOS | "when the sun had risen" | Formal; temporal clause. |
| PNEUMA | "just after sunrise" | Dynamic; compressed. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "when the sun had risen" — the genitive absolute ἀνατείλαντος τοῦ ἡλίου is a temporal marker with possible symbolic force (the sun rises as the Son has risen). LOGOS preserves the full clause. PNEUMA's "just after sunrise" is concise but loses the verbal echo of ἀνατέλλω, which can apply to both the sun and messianic rising.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 16:2 — "on the first day of the week" vs. "on Sunday morning" for τῇ μιᾷ τῶν σαββάτων

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "on the first [day] of-the week" | Literal; τῇ μιᾷ τῶν σαββάτων. |
| LOGOS | "on the first day of the week" | Formal; same. |
| PNEUMA | "on Sunday morning" | Dynamic; converts to modern day name. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "on the first day of the week" — the phrase τῇ μιᾷ τῶν σαββάτων is the standard Jewish way of naming the day after the Sabbath. "Sunday" is anachronistic in this context; the Christian naming of the day derives from this very event. Preserving "the first day of the week" maintains the Jewish temporal framework.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 16:3 — "entrance of the tomb" vs. "door of the tomb" for τῆς θύρας τοῦ μνημείου

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the door of-the tomb" | Literal; θύρα = door. |
| LOGOS | "the entrance of the tomb" | Formal; interprets θύρα contextually. |
| PNEUMA | "the entrance" | Dynamic; shortened. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the entrance of the tomb" — θύρα literally means "door," but for a rock-cut tomb sealed with a rolling stone, "entrance" is the more natural English term. "Door" (EMET) implies a hinged structure. The meaning is the same; the choice is idiomatic.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 16:4 — "it was very large" vs. "it was exceedingly great" vs. "which was massive" for ἦν γὰρ μέγας σφόδρα

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "for it-was exceedingly great" | Literal; μέγας σφόδρα = exceedingly great. |
| LOGOS | "it was very large" | Formal; natural English. |
| PNEUMA | "which was massive" | Dynamic; single vivid word. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "it was very large" — Mark's explanatory parenthetical (ἦν γὰρ μέγας σφόδρα) explains why the women were worried about the stone. "Very large" is clear and natural. "Exceedingly great" (EMET) is overly formal; "massive" (PNEUMA) is slightly hyperbolic.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 16:5 — "alarmed" vs. "amazed" vs. "stunned" for ἐξεθαμβήθησαν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they-were-amazed" | Literal; ἐκθαμβέω = be amazed/alarmed. |
| LOGOS | "they were alarmed" | Formal; emphasizes the fear component. |
| PNEUMA | "They were stunned" | Dynamic; emphasizes shock. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "they were alarmed" — ἐκθαμβέω in Mark consistently carries a component of fear/alarm alongside amazement (cf. 9:15, 14:33). The angel's immediate response — "Do not be alarmed" (μὴ ἐκθαμβεῖσθε, v. 6) — confirms that fear is the dominant note. "Amazed" (EMET) underplays the fear; "stunned" (PNEUMA) captures shock but not the ongoing emotional state.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 16:6 — "Do not be alarmed" vs. "Do not be-amazed" vs. "Don't be alarmed" for Μὴ ἐκθαμβεῖσθε

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Do not be-amazed" | Literal; matches "amazed" in v. 5. |
| LOGOS | "Do not be alarmed" | Formal; matches "alarmed" in v. 5. |
| PNEUMA | "Don't be alarmed" | Dynamic; contracted form. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Do not be alarmed" — consistent with the rendering of ἐκθαμβέω as "alarmed" in v. 5. The uncontracted "Do not" is preferred over "Don't" (PNEUMA) for the gravity of the angelic address.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 16:6 — "He has risen" vs. "He-was-raised" vs. "He has risen" for ἠγέρθη

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "He-was-raised" | Literal; ἠγέρθη is passive voice — "he was raised [by God]." |
| LOGOS | "He has risen" | Formal; traditional rendering (active sense). |
| PNEUMA | "He has risen" | Dynamic; same. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "He has risen" — this is one of the most consequential grammatical decisions in the New Testament. The Greek ἠγέρθη is aorist passive: literally, "he was raised," implying God as the agent. EMET correctly preserves the passive. However, "He has risen" is the traditional English rendering across centuries of translation (Tyndale, KJV, RSV, ESV, NIV) and carries enormous liturgical weight. The theological content — God raised Jesus — is not lost but is expressed differently: the risen Christ is the subject, not the object. The consensus follows the overwhelming weight of English translation tradition while noting EMET's grammatically faithful rendering.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA, with EMET's grammatical observation noted.

---

## 16:6 — "Jesus of Nazareth" vs. "Jesus the Nazarene" for Ἰησοῦν τὸν Ναζαρηνόν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Jesus the Nazarene" | Literal; Ναζαρηνός = Nazarene (demonym). |
| LOGOS | "Jesus of Nazareth" | Formal; traditional English form. |
| PNEUMA | "Jesus of Nazareth" | Dynamic; same. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Jesus of Nazareth" — while "the Nazarene" (EMET) is a more precise rendering of Ναζαρηνός as a demonym/title, "of Nazareth" is the established English form and functions as the standard identifier. The difference is minimal.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 16:7 — "and Peter" — rendering and emphasis for καὶ τῷ Πέτρῳ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "his disciples and Peter" | Literal; no special emphasis. |
| LOGOS | "his disciples and Peter" | Formal; same rendering. |
| PNEUMA | "his disciples — and Peter —" | Dynamic; em dashes add emphasis. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "his disciples and Peter" — all three translators agree on the content. PNEUMA's em dashes highlight Peter's separate mention, which is indeed theologically significant (as all three note in their commentary). However, the Greek has no punctuation marks; "and Peter" (καὶ τῷ Πέτρῳ) is simply appended. The emphasis is inherent in the naming itself and does not need typographical reinforcement.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 16:8 — "trembling and astonishment" vs. "shaking with shock and terror" for τρόμος καὶ ἔκστασις

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "trembling and astonishment held them" | Literal; τρόμος = trembling; ἔκστασις = astonishment/ecstasy. |
| LOGOS | "trembling and astonishment had seized them" | Formal; "seized" for εἶχεν. |
| PNEUMA | "shaking with shock and terror" | Dynamic; intensified and reworded. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "trembling and astonishment had seized them" — τρόμος καὶ ἔκστασις is a powerful word pair. "Trembling" for τρόμος is exact. "Astonishment" for ἔκστασις preserves the word's range (literally "standing outside oneself" — ecstasy, bewilderment, displacement). PNEUMA's "shock and terror" intensifies beyond the Greek: ἔκστασις is not terror but something stranger — a being-outside-oneself. "Seized" (LOGOS) is stronger than "held" (EMET) and better captures the involuntary, overwhelming nature of the experience.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 16:8 — "they said nothing to anyone" vs. "they-said nothing to-anyone" vs. "They said nothing to anyone" — final clause for οὐδενὶ οὐδὲν εἶπαν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they-said nothing to-anyone, for they-were-afraid" | Literal; double negative emphasis (οὐδενὶ οὐδέν). |
| LOGOS | "they said nothing to anyone, for they were afraid" | Formal; same. |
| PNEUMA | "They said nothing to anyone — because they were afraid" | Dynamic; dash replaces comma; "because" for γάρ. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "they said nothing to anyone, for they were afraid" — the conjunction γάρ ("for") is the last word of the Gospel of Mark. This is one of the most famous grammatical features in the New Testament. "For" preserves γάρ as a causal conjunction; "because" (PNEUMA) is semantically equivalent but lacks the archaic, formal weight appropriate to the Gospel's final word. The comma (not the dash) is the standard punctuation here.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 16:8 — "fled" vs. "rushed out and ran" for ἔφυγον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they-fled from the tomb" | Literal; φεύγω = flee. |
| LOGOS | "they went out and fled from the tomb" | Formal; preserves ἐξελθοῦσαι + ἔφυγον as two actions. |
| PNEUMA | "The women rushed out and ran from the tomb" | Dynamic; "rushed out and ran" dramatizes. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "they went out and fled from the tomb" — the Greek has two verbs: ἐξελθοῦσαι (having gone out) and ἔφυγον (they fled). LOGOS preserves both. PNEUMA's "rushed out and ran" adds urgency not present in the participle ἐξελθοῦσαι, which is a simple aorist ("having gone out"). The flight (φεύγω) is the dramatic verb; the exiting is merely sequential.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 16:9-20 — The Longer Ending: treatment and editorial note

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | Omitted; editorial note explains absence from Sinaiticus/Vaticanus | Follows manuscript evidence strictly. |
| LOGOS | Omitted; editorial note explains absence | Same approach, slightly shorter note. |
| PNEUMA | Omitted; editorial note explains absence with detailed five-point argument | Same conclusion, most extensive justification. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three translators unanimously omit vv. 9-20 based on the manuscript evidence of Sinaiticus and Vaticanus. The consensus editorial note follows LOGOS's concise approach while incorporating the key points: (1) absence from the two oldest manuscripts, (2) the later provenance of the Longer Ending, and (3) scholarly consensus. No disagreement on substance; only minor differences in the length and tone of the editorial note.

**Status:** Unanimous agreement. No resolution needed.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| **ἐκθαμβέω rendering** (alarmed vs. amazed vs. stunned) | vv. 5, 6 | "Alarmed" — fear component prioritized |
| **ἠγέρθη voice** (passive "was raised" vs. active "has risen") | v. 6 | "He has risen" — translation tradition; passive noted |
| **Time/day notation** (first day of the week vs. Sunday) | v. 2 | Ancient designation preserved |
| **PNEUMA's editorial additions** (burial, Jesus's body, em dashes, Sunday) | vv. 1, 2, 7 | Not adopted; Mark's restraint preserved |
| **Final clause register** (for vs. because; fled vs. rushed and ran) | v. 8 | Formal register for the Gospel's final words |
| **Longer Ending** (16:9-20) | vv. 9-20 | Unanimous omission |

**Overall Pattern Analysis:** Mark 16 is the shortest and most startling chapter in the Gospel — only eight verses in the earliest manuscripts. The translators show remarkable agreement, befitting the chapter's brevity and gravity. The most theologically significant disagreement concerns the voice of ἠγέρθη in v. 6: EMET's "He was raised" preserves the divine passive (God is the implied agent), while "He has risen" follows centuries of English translation tradition. Both are defensible; the consensus follows tradition while documenting the grammatical observation. The rendering of ἐκθαμβέω (vv. 5-6) matters because it sets the emotional tone of the resurrection account: Mark's women are not joyful but alarmed and afraid. PNEUMA's consistent tendency to dramatize ("rushed out and ran," "shock and terror," em dashes around "and Peter") is well-intentioned but departs from Mark's own spare, paratactic style — which is never more powerful than in these final eight verses. The unanimous agreement on omitting vv. 9-20 reflects sound textual judgment: the manuscript evidence is decisive, and all three translators handle the editorial note with scholarly integrity. Mark's Gospel ends where it began — with astonishment, silence, and an open question.
